findings.title: SQL injection: username interpolated into the query string
findings.detail: get_user() builds the SQL with an f-string, so a username like `' OR '1'='1` (or `'; DROP TABLE users;--`) is executed as SQL. This is OWASP A03 (Injection) and is directly reachable from user input.
findings.suggestion: Use a parameterized query: db.execute("SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE username = ?", (username,)).

findings.title: Privilege escalation: admin derived from email domain
findings.detail: is_admin() grants admin to anyone whose stored email ends in @admin.example.com. If users control their email at signup, they can self-assign admin. Authorization must not be inferred from user-supplied data.
findings.suggestion: Check an explicit role/flag on the user record (e.g. user['is_admin']) set by a trusted process, not the email domain.

findings.title: Unhandled missing row raises TypeError
findings.detail: When the username doesn't exist, cur.fetchone() returns None, so row[0] raises `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not subscriptable` instead of a clean not-found result.
findings.suggestion: Guard the None case: `if row is None: raise UserNotFound(username)` (or return None) before indexing.

findings.title: No test covers the not-found or admin paths
findings.detail: Two new branches ship untested: get_user() on a missing username, and is_admin() true/false. A regression in either would pass CI silently.
findings.suggestion: Add tests: get_user() raises/returns on unknown user; is_admin() is False for a normal email and only True via an explicit role.
